stem: A 72-year-old male with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with acute right-sided weakness and slurred speech that began 90 minutes ago. A non-contrast head CT is negative for hemorrhage, and his NIHSS score is 12. Which action should the nurse prioritize at this time?

options.label: Administer intravenous alteplase as prescribed.
options.hoverRationale: Alteplase is a thrombolytic that dissolves the fibrin matrix of the clot causing the ischemic stroke. Restoring blood flow within the critical 3 to 4.5-hour window can significantly reduce neurological deficits and improve long-term outcomes.

options.label: Request an urgent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan.
options.hoverRationale: The clinical flaw is delaying time-sensitive treatment. While an MRI provides detailed images, a non-contrast CT is sufficient to rule out hemorrhage, which is the only imaging required before administering alteplase in an acute ischemic stroke.

options.label: Obtain a neurology consultation before initiating treatment.
options.hoverRationale: The clinical flaw is causing an unnecessary delay. While a neurologist is part of the stroke team, waiting for a formal consultation before acting on established stroke protocols can waste critical time, as 'time is brain.'

options.label: Administer labetalol for a blood pressure of 175/95 mmHg.
options.hoverRationale: The clinical flaw is misinterpreting blood pressure guidelines. Permissive hypertension (up to 185/110 mmHg) is allowed before administering alteplase to maintain cerebral perfusion. Lowering the blood pressure unnecessarily could worsen ischemic injury.

rationale.correct: The patient is experiencing an acute ischemic stroke and is within the 3 to 4.5-hour window for thrombolytic therapy. The non-contrast CT scan has ruled out hemorrhage, making the administration of intravenous alteplase the priority to restore cerebral perfusion and minimize brain injury.

mnemonic.BEFAST: Balance, Eyes, Face, Arms, Speech, Time (to call 911). This mnemonic helps in the rapid identification of stroke symptoms, emphasizing the urgency of seeking immediate medical attention.

distractors: Delaying thrombolysis for advanced imaging like an MRI.
Prioritizing a non-urgent consultation over immediate, protocol-driven care.
Treating permissive hypertension, which is a protective mechanism in acute stroke.
commonMistakes: Failing to recognize the urgency of the therapeutic window for alteplase.
Misunderstanding the role of a non-contrast CT as the primary decision-making tool for thrombolysis.
Incorrectly applying blood pressure management guidelines for acute ischemic stroke.

clinicalPearls: Time is brain in acute stroke management; the therapeutic window for IV alteplase is typically 3 to 4.5 hours from symptom onset.
A non-contrast head CT is the essential first imaging study to differentiate between ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke.
Permissive hypertension (typically up to 185/110 mmHg) is often allowed before and during the first 24 hours after alteplase administration to maintain cerebral perfusion.

itemContext.sbar: S: 72-year-old male with acute right-sided weakness and slurred speech. B: History of A-fib, HTN. Last known well 07:30, arrival 09:00. A: NIHSS 12. Non-contrast head CT is negative. BP 175/95 mmHg. R: Recommend initiating alteplase protocol.

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:
• Blood Pressure: 175/95 mmHg
  • Heart Rate: 92/min (irregular)
  • Respiratory Rate: 18/min
  • Temperature: 98.6°F (37°C)
  • Oxygen Saturation: 97% on room air

patient.currentComplaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech at 08:00. Last known well was at 07:30.
